Gandikota

[Telugu History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/telugu-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Gandikota

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Aug 24, 2021

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r8KC!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Feafe5374-c65e-40a2-a176-c6d3dabfbde2_360x270.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ca0B!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa4212dc5-ed9a-4a85-a5cb-0126208a62c0_324x155.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ca0B!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa4212dc5-ed9a-4a85-a5cb-0126208a62c0_324x155.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!uSTU!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fdb710830-204e-4905-a634-d9ad3f0d5bd8_360x288.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!uSTU!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fdb710830-204e-4905-a634-d9ad3f0d5bd8_360x288.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Perched on the banks of the Penna river, Gandikota is often celebrated today for its breathtaking gorge, aptly earning the title “Grand Canyon of India.” The name itself derives from *gandi*, meaning “gorge,” a reference to the dramatic 300-foot-wide gap carved by the Penna as it winds through the Erramala hills. The sight of the river cutting through red granite cliffs creates a landscape of awe-inspiring beauty, drawing travelers and photographers alike.

Yet Gandikota is more than a geological wonder. Its fort, built in the 13th century, stands as a silent sentinel to centuries of valor and cultural synthesis. Under the stewardship of the Pemmasani Nayakas, the fort became a formidable bastion of the Vijayanagara Empire. The Nayakas, renowned as the “sword arm” of the empire, defended its frontiers with unmatched skill and courage. Within the fort’s sprawling walls lie temples dedicated to Ranganatha and Madhavaraya, a mosque, granaries, and intricate structures that speak to the architectural and spiritual richness of its past.

While the canyon itself is splendid in its own way, the history of Gandikota Fort is equally captivating. Rising above the Penna river gorge, the fort has long stood as a testament to resilience and martial prowess. Its very name has become synonymous with bravery and courage, much like the legendary strongholds of Bobbili and Kondaveedu.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AJRz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9b76064c-214d-4119-88c3-6558e206fcba_512x331.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AJRz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9b76064c-214d-4119-88c3-6558e206fcba_512x331.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The story of Gandikota begins long before its rise to fame as the “Grand Canyon of India.” The original fort was constructed in 1123 by a local chieftain under the Kalyani Chalukya ruler Someswara I. Unlike the imposing stone ramparts seen today, this early fort was built of sand, marking the humble beginnings of what would later become a formidable bastion.

Between 1239 and 1304, Gandikota came under the rule of the Kakatiyas, a dynasty renowned for its patronage of art, architecture, and military might. Their stewardship added strength and permanence to the fort, laying the foundation for its later prominence under the Vijayanagara Empire.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zY4I!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F88dff2b1-07ea-411e-aa56-769177d5159e_512x331.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zY4I!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F88dff2b1-07ea-411e-aa56-769177d5159e_512x331.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cZm1!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbba78045-26d9-4580-9abb-56f6853d56d4_240x155.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cZm1!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbba78045-26d9-4580-9abb-56f6853d56d4_240x155.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xkss!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F05d6ac42-5313-47ec-9228-fa5d226db3f8_240x142.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xkss!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F05d6ac42-5313-47ec-9228-fa5d226db3f8_240x142.jpeg)

The grandeur of Gandikota Fort reached its zenith under the Pemmasani Nayakas, whose valor and loyalty to the Vijayanagara Empire became legendary. The Pemmasani kingdom was founded by Pemmasani Timma Nayaka when Sri Krishnadeva Raya entrusted him with the Tadipatri province around 1509. This marked the beginning of a dynasty that would become the empire’s “sword arm,” renowned for its military prowess and indomitable spirit.

The current fort at Gandikota was built by Pemmasani Ramalinga Nayaka, commander of the Vijayanagara Army and son of Pemmasani Timmanayudu II. Ramalinga Nayaka was celebrated as a great warrior, and under his leadership, Gandikota became not only a formidable stronghold but also a symbol of courage and resilience. The fort’s massive stone walls, temples, and defensive structures reflected both strategic brilliance and cultural richness, embodying the spirit of the empire at its height.

Ramalinga Nayaka played a decisive role in Sri Krishnadeva Raya’s victories over the Bahmani Sultans of Golkonda and Bijapur. His leadership at the Battle of Raichur, one of the greatest triumphs in Vijayanagara’s history, cemented his reputation as a warrior who led from the front and inspired his men with courage and skill.

Recognizing Gandikota’s strategic and symbolic importance, Ramalinga Nayaka made it the capital of his dominion. From here, he ruled over a vast territory that stretched across Tadipatri, Gutti, Kondaveedu, and extended all the way to Kondapalli. Under his stewardship, Gandikota was transformed into a formidable stronghold and a center of power, embodying both military strength and cultural richness.

The Pemmasanis continued to play a crucial role even after Sri Krishnadeva Raya’s reign, becoming instrumental in the rise of the Aravidu dynasty. Their influence ensured that Gandikota remained not just a fortress, but a synonym for valor and resilience in South Indian history.

The glory of Gandikota eventually waned with the fall of the Pemmasani Nayakas. In 1652 AD, Pemmasani Timma Nayaka, the last ruler of Gandikota, was defeated by Mir Jumla, the general of the Golkonda Qutub Shahis. With this, the fort passed into the hands of the Qutub Shahi dynasty, marking the end of Pemmasani dominance. In the centuries that followed, Gandikota came under various rulers, eventually falling into British control during the colonial era.

Within the fort’s walls stand two temples dedicated to Madhavaraya and Ranganatha. The Madhavaraya temple, built during the reign of Sri Krishnadeva Raya, remains a striking example of Vijayanagara architecture, though its main deity was later shifted to Mydukuru after the fort’s capture by the Golkonda Sultans. The Ranganatha temple, though more ruined today, still whispers of the fort’s spiritual and cultural richness.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tHd4!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fedce4b42-ec03-457a-8c62-b397494d6fd4_240x143.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tHd4!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fedce4b42-ec03-457a-8c62-b397494d6fd4_240x143.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qVFJ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F49aae8d8-4f66-4d98-9eb0-e54577b95379_240x180.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qVFJ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F49aae8d8-4f66-4d98-9eb0-e54577b95379_240x180.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rpF0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1dbc41c2-31f3-41ed-baad-e2c532d03679_240x180.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rpF0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1dbc41c2-31f3-41ed-baad-e2c532d03679_240x180.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZX0z!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6529b1a5-1ecd-489b-bfb1-8f8eca2d6788_355x142.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZX0z!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6529b1a5-1ecd-489b-bfb1-8f8eca2d6788_355x142.jpeg)

The **Madhavaraya Temple**, built during the reign of Sri Krishnadeva Raya, is especially renowned for its ornate *mandapa* and a towering five-storey *gopuram*. The gateway is adorned with intricate sculptures, while the temple’s wide courtyard reflects the scale and artistry of Vijayanagara architecture. Though the main deity was later shifted to Mydukuru after the fort’s capture by the Golkonda Sultans, the temple remains a striking reminder of Gandikota’s spiritual and cultural vitality.

The fort also housed vital structures that sustained its role as a stronghold. A **large granary with a vaulted roof** once stored food grains, ensuring resilience during sieges. An **ammunition magazine**, now mostly in ruins, speaks to its military preparedness. There was also a **palace**, though little of it survives today, hinting at the grandeur that once animated the fort’s inner life.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bLRw!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8076c7ad-062a-4404-9b04-243fa2146cef_259x194.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bLRw!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8076c7ad-062a-4404-9b04-243fa2146cef_259x194.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cHNV!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa0fa1388-4dac-4bd5-a867-43ae5d2a3085_240x160.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cHNV!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa0fa1388-4dac-4bd5-a867-43ae5d2a3085_240x160.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Q1p6!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d12574a-f939-4064-8a7e-fe9b99220b19_240x180.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Q1p6!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d12574a-f939-4064-8a7e-fe9b99220b19_240x180.jpeg)

Gandikota Fort is not only a marvel of military architecture but also home to several intriguing structures that enrich its historical character. Among them is the **House of Drums**, a hall believed to have been used for ceremonial or martial purposes, echoing with the rhythms of the fort’s vibrant past.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZR8i!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2f969206-1039-4685-bf6a-665b17a3b22a_287x175.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZR8i!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2f969206-1039-4685-bf6a-665b17a3b22a_287x175.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZWse!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fedddba26-7ea9-472c-ba5e-57199db1b0a6_240x134.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZWse!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fedddba26-7ea9-472c-ba5e-57199db1b0a6_240x134.jpeg)

A **Charminar-like structure** stands within the premises, reflecting the architectural influences that swept through the Deccan during successive dynasties. The fort also contains a **prison**, a stark reminder of its role as a seat of power and authority.

Adding to its mystique is the **Erra Koneru**, a lake whose name translates to “Red Lake.” Local lore holds that warriors once washed their blood-stained swords here after battle, turning its waters red,a haunting symbol of Gandikota’s martial legacy. Within the fort also lies the **Jamia Masjid**, built during the reign of the Golkonda Sultans. Its graceful arches and spacious courtyard stand as testimony to the cultural and religious diversity that marked Gandikota’s history.
